claims.name: samskara_gives
claims.cite: that is the pattern for sound

rejected_readings.label: sound_determines_object_alone
rejected_readings.rendering: Sound operates only to manifest the word directly; it does not condition the hearer's faculty at all.
rejected_readings.why: The verse establishes that sound works like light: conditioning both receiver and object. Denying the receiver-side (hearer's faculty conditioning) severs the complete analogy and misses the phenomenology of how repetition ripens understanding.

rejected_readings.label: light_determines_object_alone
rejected_readings.rendering: In vision, light only makes the object visible; it does not prepare the eye's capacity to see.
rejected_readings.why: The commentary says light plays a double role, preparing both eye and object. If light worked only on the object, the analogy to sound would not establish that sound conditions both hearer and word.

rejected_readings.label: sound_essence_equals_light
rejected_readings.rendering: Sound and light are essentially the same substance, differing only in their sensory appearance.
rejected_readings.why: The commentary explicitly defers the question of whether sound is separately perceived (1.81). Identifying them in substance would prematurely close a debate that Bhartrhari intentionally leaves open. The verse establishes only a functional parallel, not an identity.

notes: Verse 1.80 establishes the double-conditioning model: as light prepares both the eye and the object in vision, sound (dhvani) conditions both the hearer's faculty and the word's manifestation in audition. This two-sided samskara is critical to Bhartṛhari's phonological theory. The metaphysical question of whether dhvani is separately perceived remains open (1.81).
